title: Keyboard Walk
subtitle: Every normal-mode key, organized by physical key location.
summary: A row-by-row tour of the QWERTY keyboard: every unshifted key, every shifted key, with a note on Ctrl- variants. Useful as a wall poster and as a study plan.

blocks.text: Vim's bindings cover *every key on the keyboard*. Not most of them. Not the popular ones. **Every key.** Hold a finger over almost any character on a QWERTY keyboard, hit it in *normal mode*, and something happens — a motion, an operator, a prefix into a family of commands, a jump, an action. The empty key is the exception, not the rule.

That's also a study plan. You don't have to learn Vim's grammar all at once — you can *walk the keyboard*. Pick a key a day, hit it in *normal mode*, see what it does, look it up. After a few weeks the unshifted letters are second nature. Then shifted letters. Then shifted symbols. Then a handful of useful Ctrl- variants. By the time you've finished one full lap of the keyboard, you've absorbed essentially every common normal-mode command in Vim.

The order of importance:

  1. **Unshifted keys** — the bulk of day-to-day editing. Motions ({key:h}{key:j}{key:k}{key:l}, {key:w}, {key:b}, {key:e}, {key:f}, {key:t}), operators ({key:d}, {key:c}, {key:y}), modes ({key:i}, {key:a}, {key:o}, {key:v}, {key::}), basic editing ({key:u}, {key:x}, {key:p}, {key:r}, {key:s}).
  2. **Shifted keys** — bigger, opposite, or specialised siblings of the unshifted versions. {key:A} appends at end of line, {key:O} opens above, {key:I} inserts at first non-blank, {key:N} reverses search, {key:G} jumps to last line.
  3. **Shifted symbols** — search-word-under-cursor ({key:*}, {key:#}), sentence motions ({key:(}, {key:)}), paragraph motions ({key:{}, {key:}}), indent operators ({key:>}, {key:<}), end-of-line ({key:$}), bracket-match ({key:%}).
  4. **Ctrl- variants** — window prefixes ({key:Ctrl-W}…), scrolling ({key:Ctrl-D}, {key:Ctrl-U}, {key:Ctrl-F}, {key:Ctrl-B}), the jumplist ({key:Ctrl-O}, {key:Ctrl-I}), inc/dec ({key:Ctrl-A}, {key:Ctrl-X}), redo ({key:Ctrl-R}), file-info ({key:Ctrl-G}). A full reference of every Ctrl- key lives in [Complete Key Reference](#appendix.complete-key-reference).

If you're just starting out: walk the unshifted keys first, in any order. {key:h}{key:j}{key:k}{key:l} obviously, but also pick the strange ones — {key:t}, {key:s}, {key:q}, {key:m}, {key:z}. Most of them either *do* something useful immediately or are *prefixes* into a family worth learning.

rows: Jump to mark (precise column) — {key:`}{key:a} jumps to mark `a`, {key:`}{key:`} to last position
Up to first non-blank of previous line
Record macro into register (`qa`…`q`); replay with `@a`
Till char on line (motion — stops *before* match)
Open new line below and insert
Backward-prefix family (`[c` prev hunk, `[m` prev method, `[{` prev `{`)
Find char on line forward (motion — lands *on* match)
Jump to mark (line, first non-blank) — {key:'}{key:a} jumps to mark `a`, {key:'}{key:'} to last position
Prefix family (`zz` centre, `zt` top, `zb` bottom, `zf` fold, `zo`/`zc` open/close fold, `zM`/`zR` close/open all)
Set mark (`ma` marks current position as `a`)
Down to first non-blank of next line (same as `+`)

blocks.text: Once the unshifted keys feel automatic, walk the keyboard again with shift held. The pattern: shifted-letter = *bigger / opposite / line-flavored* version of its unshifted counterpart. Shifted-number = a punctuation symbol with its own meaning. Shifted-punctuation = often a paired motion or an operator.

rows: Toggle case of character under cursor (advances cursor)
Filter operator — run buffer range through external shell command (`!ap`)
Play macro from register (`@a` plays register `a`, `@@` replays last)
Search backward for word under cursor
Jump to matching `(`/`)`/`[`/`]`/`{`/`}`; also %-range in Ex
Repeat last `:s` substitution on current line
Search forward for word under cursor
Down to first non-blank (same as `-`, but next line)
Down to first non-blank of next line (same as Enter)
In Neovim: replay last macro. In classic Vim: enter *ex mode*
Yank line (classic Vim) / yank to end of line (Neovim default)
Undo all changes on current line (rarely used)
Insert at first non-blank of line
Lookup keyword under cursor (`man`, `:help`, or LSP hover)
Enter *command-line mode* — type an Ex command
Register prefix (`"ap` paste from register `a`, `"+y` yank to system clipboard)
Save/quit prefix (`ZZ` save + quit, `ZQ` quit without saving)

blocks.text: Most letters also have a Ctrl- variant. Many of them are useful, a handful are essential, and a few do nothing in *normal mode* at all. The most worthwhile to learn:

rows: Jumplist backward — return to previous cursor location
Increment number under or after cursor
Decrement number under or after cursor
Show file info (path, line number, total lines)
Jump to tag under cursor (or LSP definition with plugin)

blocks.text: Every other Ctrl- combination — including the obscure and the rarely-useful — is catalogued in [Complete Key Reference](#appendix.complete-key-reference). When in doubt about what {key:Ctrl-X}, say, does in some context, look it up there.

A few Ctrl- keys are stolen by the terminal or window manager before Vim sees them: {key:Ctrl-S} (XON flow control), {key:Ctrl-Q} (XOFF), {key:Ctrl-Z} (suspend), {key:Ctrl-C} (interrupt). If a mapping doesn't fire, that's the first thing to check.
